title: Cuts & Scrapes
description: How to treat superficial scrapes, deeper cuts, and wounds with foreign objects
steps: Rinse the wound and surrounding skin with clean water or saline solution
Disinfect the wound and 2-3cm surrounding area with iodine/povidone-iodine
Apply a bandage for small wounds; cover larger wounds with sterile gauze
Change dressings daily and monitor healing progress
warnings: Do not apply alcohol directly to open wounds (too irritating)
Do not apply toothpaste, soy sauce, herbal powders, or home remedies
Do not force removal of deeply embedded foreign objects
Do not pick at scabs on healing wounds
whenToSeeDoctor: Wound depth exceeds 1 cm or exposes fat/muscle tissue
Wound edges are ragged and won't close together
Bleeding continues after 10 minutes of firm pressure
Puncture from rusty metal or dirty objects (may need tetanus shot)
Signs of infection: increasing redness, swelling, heat, pain, or pus

title: Burns & Scalds
description: Treatment for thermal burns, flame burns, and chemical burns
steps: COOL: Run cool to cold flowing water over the burn for at least 15-20 minutes
REMOVE: Carefully remove clothing while under cool water; cut away stuck fabric, don't pull it off
SOAK: Continue soaking the burn in cool water for 10-20 minutes
COVER: Gently cover the burn with clean gauze or sterile dressing
SEEK HELP: Get to a hospital or call emergency services as soon as possible
warnings: Do NOT apply toothpaste, soy sauce, cooking oil, ash, or home remedies
Do NOT pop blisters
Do NOT apply ice directly (can cause frostbite damage)
For chemical burns: wipe away residue first before rinsing (to avoid spreading)
whenToSeeDoctor: Burn area larger than the palm of your hand
Burns on face, hands, joints, or genitals
Second-degree burns or worse (blisters, white/charred skin)
Chemical burns or electrical burns
Signs of shock: pale skin, cold sweat, weak rapid pulse

title: Sprains & Fractures
description: Joint sprains, suspected fractures, spinal injury handling and transport precautions
steps: REST: Stop activity immediately; do not bear weight on the injury
ICE: Apply an ice pack wrapped in a towel for 15-20 minutes at a time
COMPRESS: Wrap with an elastic bandage for support (not too tight)
ELEVATE: Raise the injured limb above heart level
warnings: If fracture is suspected, do NOT attempt to realign or force movement
For spine/neck injuries, do NOT move the patient arbitrarily
Do NOT apply heat during the acute phase (first 48 hours)
Do NOT massage or rub the injured area
whenToSeeDoctor: Visible deformity or abnormal movement in the limb
Unable to bear weight or severely limited range of motion
Severe pain and rapidly worsening swelling
Suspected head or spinal injury
Signs of circulatory problems: numbness, pale or cold skin

title: Animal & Insect Bites
description: Dog bites, cat scratches, snake bites, bee stings, tick bites treatment
steps: Dog/Cat bite: Immediately wash wound with soap and running water alternately for 15 minutes
Snake bite: Stay calm, immobilize the limb, apply a bandage above the bite site
Bee sting: Scrape out the stinger with fingernail or card (don't squeeze venom sac), rinse with water
Tick bite: Use fine-tipped tweezers to grasp tick close to skin and pull straight out
warnings: Do NOT immediately suture dog bite wounds (allows drainage)
Do NOT suck out snake venom with mouth or cut the wound
Do NOT squeeze bee stinger (injects more venom)
Do NOT yank ticks forcefully (may leave mouthparts embedded)
Do NOT ignore any animal bite (rabies risk)
whenToSeeDoctor: ANY dog/cat bite (rabies vaccination required)
Venomous snake bite (antivenom needed)
Multiple bee stings or allergic reaction (difficulty breathing, widespread rash)
Tick bite followed by fever or rash (Lyme disease risk)
Wound becomes red, swollen, or shows signs of infection

title: Foreign Objects in Eye
description: Treatment for dust, insects, or chemicals splashed into the eye
steps: Dust/insect: Blink repeatedly to let tears flush it out
Gently pull eyelid and slowly flush eye with saline or clean water
Chemical splash: Immediately flush with large amounts of flowing water for at least 15 minutes (hold eyelids open)
If discomfort persists after rinsing, loosely cover both eyes with clean gauze
warnings: Do NOT rub your eye (may scratch the cornea)
Do NOT use dirty hands or tissues to wipe the eyeball
For chemical burns: Do NOT apply eye drops before thorough rinsing
Do NOT try to remove embedded objects yourself
whenToSeeDoctor: Chemical splashes into eye (especially acids or alkalis)
Foreign body sensation persists longer than 30 minutes
Blurred vision, light sensitivity, or excessive tearing
Severe eye pain or heavy redness
Sharp object has penetrated or is embedded in the eye

title: Frostbite
description: Mild frostbite, severe hypothermia, rewarming precautions
steps: Move to a warm environment and remove wet/cold clothing
Mild frostbite: Soak affected area in warm water (37-40°C / 98-104°F), NOT hot water
Use body heat to rewarm (e.g., hold against armpit or abdomen)
After rewarming, gently protect area with sterile gauze
warnings: Do NOT rub frostbitten area with snow (causes more tissue damage)
Do NOT use hot water or fire to rewarm directly (can cause further damage)
For severe hypothermia: do NOT rewarm too quickly (risk of arrhythmia)
Do NOT massage or pound frozen areas
whenToSeeDoctor: Skin feels hard, turns white or yellow (deep tissue damage)
Large-area frostbite or whole-body hypothermia
Sensation does not return or blisters form after rewarming
Confusion, slurred speech, or other hypothermia symptoms
Frostbitten area turns dark purple or shows necrosis signs
